items.q: What did the original Phillips curve suggest, and how has thinking changed?
items.correct: The original Phillips curve suggested a stable inverse relationship between unemployment and inflation. Friedman and the 1970s stagflation experience added expectations — the long-run Phillips curve is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment.
items.misconception: The Phillips curve still proves you can always reduce unemployment by allowing more inflation.
items.random_wrong: The Phillips curve shows the link between phone use and unemployment.
items.why: The simple inverse trade-off broke down empirically. Modern theory distinguishes short-run (trade-off possible) from long-run (no trade-off). Students learn the original curve as if still valid.

items.q: What is the difference between GDP and GNP?
items.correct: GDP measures output produced within a country's borders; GNP measures output produced by a country's nationals wherever they are.
items.misconception: GDP and GNP are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: GDP is income and GNP is spending.
items.why: Foreign profits from a UK firm abroad count in UK GNP but not UK GDP. Profits of a Japanese firm in the UK count in UK GDP but Japan's GNP. The distinction matters for globalised economies.

items.q: What is the predicted effect of a binding price ceiling like rent control?
items.correct: Shortage in the market — quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at the controlled price, leading to queues, black markets and reduced quality.
items.misconception: Price ceilings make goods cheaper for everyone with no downsides.
items.random_wrong: Price ceilings only affect the rich.
items.why: Students see price control as obviously beneficial. Standard analysis predicts shortages and unintended consequences — the trade-off is central to evaluation questions on intervention.
Correct answer: MR = MC. “P = MC” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: LRAC keeps falling. “LRAC rises” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Exact answer: 50. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
